perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall provide the Confidential Information only to those of its employees and directors (and any agents, representatives, consultants, or financial advisors that have been approved in advance by the Disclosing Party) that have a bona fide need to know for the Purpose, and in the case of AST to any of its current or prospective member companies, in each case who are bound by a written agreement including confidentiality and non-use terms 2 sd-409605 v4 and conditions at least as protective as those in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall provide the Confidential Information only to those of its employees and directors (and any agents, representatives, consultants, or financial advisors that have been approved in advance by the Disclosing Party) that have a bona fide need to know for the Purpose, and in the case of AST to any of its current or prospective member companies. The Receiving Party shall not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than the Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'in each case who are bound by a written agreement including confidentiality and non-use terms 2 sd-409605 v4 and conditions at least as protective as those in this Agreement.' it permits the receiving party to disclose to members or employees without need for written agreements. This reduces liability of receiving party and makes the confidentiality clause less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Delaware Trade Secret Act aims to protect trade secrets, which can include confidential information covered by NDAs. By removing the requirement for written confidentiality agreements with employees and member companies, the modified text weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially violating the spirit, and potentially the legal requirements, of the Delaware Trade Secret Act depending on the specific nature of the confidential information and the context of its use or disclosure.

Trade Secrets Â§ 2001. Definitions. The following words, terms and phrases, when used in this chapter, shall have the meanings ascribed to them in this section, except where the context clearly indicates a different meaning: (1) âImproper meansâ shall include theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) âMisappropriationâ shall mean: a. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or b. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: 1. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or 2. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was: A. Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; B. Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or C. Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or 3. Before a material change of the personâs position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) âPersonâ shall mean a natural person, corporation, statutory trust, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) âTrade secretâ shall mean information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that: a. Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement that the Receiving Party's employees, directors, agents, representatives, consultants, financial advisors, and AST member companies be bound by written confidentiality agreements. The Law Explanation asserts that this weakens the protection of confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, defining "Misappropriation" under the Delaware Trade Secret Act, includes "Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person...[who] knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade was...Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use." By removing the requirement for written confidentiality agreements, the modified text makes it harder to establish that a duty to maintain secrecy exists. This creates a structural flaw in the contract that diminishes the enforceability of the confidentiality clause and creates a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of the Receiving Party under Section 2 shall survive termination of this Agreement and shall remain in effect until the earlier of: (a) the Disclosing Party, in its sole discretion, releases the Receiving Party by notification in writing that the Receiving Party is no longer required to protect the Confidential Information pursuant to this Agreement; or (b) the Confidential Information falls within an exception set forth in Section 3; or (c) two (2) years from the date of disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of the Receiving Party under Section 2 shall survive termination of this Agreement and shall remain in effect until the earlier of: (a) the Disclosing Party, in its sole discretion, releases the Receiving Party by notification in writing that the Receiving Party is no longer required to protect the Confidential Information pursuant to this Agreement; or (b) the Confidential Information falls within an exception set forth in Section 3.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'two (2) years from the date of disclosure of the Confidential Information' clause, the reader is mislead into thinking there's no time limitation. Removing an objectively measured time constraint could be deemed indefinite, which is hard to enforce.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Contract Law - Definiteness of Terms
perturbation.law_citation: Osborn ex rel. Osborn v. Kemp, 991 A.2d 1153, 1159 (Del. 2010)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware contract law requires that agreements have definite and certain terms.  Removing the two-year time limit for confidentiality could render the confidentiality obligation indefinite, making it potentially unenforceable under Delaware law. A key component of contract enforcement is having clear and measurable terms, without it the contract can fail due to indefiniteness.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a definite time constraint (two years) for confidentiality, potentially rendering the obligation indefinite. The Law Explanation states Delaware contract law requires definite and certain terms, and removing the time limit makes the confidentiality obligation indefinite, making it potentially unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates a file not found, but the Law Explanation provides enough evidence to confirm this assertion.
